issue_title: Make host setting consistent
issue_body:
There's a minor discrepancy in how the hostname is lowercased, based on which API you use.

##### Steps to replicate

This seems OK:

```python
>>> url = furl('https://MyHostWithCaps.example.org')
>>> url.host
'myhostwithcaps.example.org'
>>> str(url)
'https://myhostwithcaps.example.org'
```

This seems not OK (or at least not consistent):

```python
>>> url = furl('https://MyHostWithCaps.example.org')
>>> url.host = 'MyHostWithCaps.example.org'             # <- difference here!
>>> url.host
'MyHostWithCaps.example.org'
>>> str(url)
'https://MyHostWithCaps.example.org'
```

##### Expected result

The `u.host` property should _always_ return a lowercased value.

##### Actual result

The `u.host` property can _sometimes_ return a string with non-lowercase chars.

issue_title: gabbi-run does not expose failures' originating suite
issue_body:
to reproduce:

    $ python -m gabbi.runner http://example.org -- gabbi/tests/gabbits_runner/*.yaml

(which corresponds to `gabbi-run … -- /path/to/*.yaml`)

this results in three failures from three separate suites:

```
... ✗ expected failure

FAIL: expected failure
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    …
    AssertionError: '301' not found in ['666'], response:
    …
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.183s

FAILED (failures=1)
... ✗ expected success

FAIL: expected success
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    …
    AssertionError: '301' not found in ['200'], response:
    …
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.065s

FAILED (failures=1)
... ✗ expected success

FAIL: expected success
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    …
    AssertionError: '301' not found in ['200'], response:
    …
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.060s

FAILED (failures=1)
```

Other than the respective test's `name`, there's barely any indication where a failure originated.

Somewhat relatedly, there's also no summary across all suites - so a casual observer might only spot the last report, erroneously concluding there was only a single failure (or worse: if the last suite did _not_ fail, assume everything's fine).

issue_title: Add sourceFileMap support
issue_body:
##### Describe the issue
We build our programs in a docker environment, and so the source files in the debug symbols all have a docker filepath prefix. It would be nice to be able to provide a sourceFileMap like [VSCode allows](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-cpptools/blob/master/launch.md) to map the docker path to the real path. For example:

`./gdbgui_0.10.1.0 -g /opt/linaro/5.2.1/bin/aarch64-linux-gnu-gdb -m "/docker/buildenv":"/real/path"`

I am able to work around the issue by setting the working directory in `gdb` which then allows gdb to use relative paths instead of absolute ones.

##### Steps to replicate

* Step 1

Build binary in docker container

* Step 2

Debug binary outside of docker container

##### Describe your environment
* Operating system and version: Ubuntu 16.10
* gdbgui version (`gdbgui -v`): 0.10.1.0

issue_title: Define NOMINMAX before windows.h
issue_body:
I ran into an issue where GLAD included `windows.h`.  I've read #42, and I understand why you thought it was a good idea to keep it, but one thing that would help a whole bunch is defining `NOMINMAX` before including `windows.h`.  This specifically is a major pain-point when including `windows.h`, as the min and max macros conflict with `std::min` and `std::max`, which seems like behavior you would want to avoid in most cases.  Those who needed Microsoft's min/max could simply include `windows.h` before GLAD.

For what it's worth, `SDL_opengl.h` appears to do exactly what I describe.

issue_title: Incorrect answers with numpy ufuncs and the out keyword
issue_body:
Consider the following example:

```python
import numpy as np
from pint import UnitRegistry

u = UnitRegistry()

a = [1, 2, 3]*u.g
b = [1, 2, 3]*u.kg
out = np.zeros(3)

print(np.add(a, b, out=out))
```

With `Pint 0.8.1`, I get `[2. 4. 6.]` as the output of this script, when I would expect either `[1001. 1002. 1003.]`, `[1.001 1.002 1.003]` or similar with units attached. `Pint` returns the correct answer when `out` is not specified.

issue_title: Param switches on faker.password() don't guarantee valid password
issue_body:
The format switches on `faker.password()` (`special_chars, digits, upper_case, lower_case`) don't always return passwords matching those rules.

This is problematic as when using generated passwords in unit tests, where passwords must conform to validity rules (e.g. "must contain numbers"), tests can randomly fail.

I expected that these switches would guarantee the function returns a conforming password. e.g. `faker.password(digits=True)` always returns a password containing digits, but this is not the case.

issue_title: Support for C.H.I.P.
issue_body:
Hi,

First of all, great library! I use it to control my porch light through a component in [Home Assitant](https://home-assistant.io). I recently migrated my setup from a RPi to a [C.H.I.P.](https://getchip.com) and noticed that this package does not work anymore. This because `RPi.GPIO ` of course only works on a RPi. However there is a (almost) drop-in replacement for this dependency called [CHIP_IO](https://github.com/xtacocorex/CHIP_IO). The only method that CHIP_IO seems to lack is `GPIO.setmode`.

With this patch a got the library working on the C.H.I.P.:

```diff
diff --git a/rpi_rf/rpi_rf.py b/rpi_rf/rpi_rf.py
index 3949169..72c1e70 100644
--- a/rpi_rf/rpi_rf.py
+++ b/rpi_rf/rpi_rf.py
@@ -6,7 +6,10 @@ import logging
 import time
 from collections import namedtuple

-from RPi import GPIO
+try:
+    from RPi import GPIO
+except:
+    from CHIP_IO import GPIO

 MAX_CHANGES = 67

@@ -54,8 +57,11 @@ class RFDevice:
         self.rx_proto = None
         self.rx_bitlength = None
         self.rx_pulselength = None
-
-        GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
+        # the method 'setmode' is not available in CHIP_IO.GPIO
+        try:
+            GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
+        except:
+            pass
         _LOGGER.debug("Using GPIO " + str(gpio))

     def cleanup(self):
```

If you are willing to add support for C.H.I.P. I could make a PR out of this patch.

Cheers!

issue_title: problem using `-cflags …` and `-mklib`
issue_body:
It seems that using `cflags '...'` together with `-mklib` causes the `-c` flag that `-mklib` inserts to evaporate.

For example:

```
FoBiS.py build -ch -cflags '-O0' -mklib static -s src -o libjsonfortran.a -t src/json_module.F90 -colors
The present cflags are different from the heritages one: forcing to (re-)compile all
Builder options
  Directories
    Building directory: "."
    Compiled-objects .o   directory: "obj"
    Compiled-objects .mod directory: "mod"
  Compiler options
    Vendor: "intel"
    Compiler command: "ifort"
    Module directory switch: "-module"
    Compiling flags: "-O0"
    Linking flags: ""
    Preprocessing flags: ""
    Coverage: False
    Profile: False
  PreForM.py used: False
  PreForM.py output directory: None
  PreForM.py extensions processed: []

Building src/json_module.F90
Compiling src/json_module.F90 serially
Undefined symbols for architecture x86_64:
  "_MAIN__", referenced from:
      _main in for_main.o
ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture x86_64
```
 A work around is to manually add the `-c` to the `-cflags` but this should be done automatically whenever `-mklib` is used, even when adding custom flags.

Also, one still needs to put a space in the front of the flags string passed to the `-cflags` switch, otherwise a usage message appears.

issue_body:
I noticed this while troubleshooting LOCK issues with wsgidav.
apparently once the LOCK is requested, in the returned xml, Seconds has apparently negative value visible in the real response below (-1488454203)

request:
`<?xml` version="1.0" encoding="utf-8" ?>.<D:lockinfo `xmlns:D="DAV:">.<D:lockscope><D:exclusive/></D:lockscope>.<D:locktype><D:write/></D:locktype>.<D:owner>.<D:href>mailto:xxx.xxxxx@xxxxxx.xxxx</D:href>.</D:owner>.</D:lockinfo>`

response:
`<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>.<ns0:prop xmlns:ns0="DAV:"><ns0:lockdiscovery><ns0:activelock><ns0:locktype><ns0:write /></ns0:locktype><ns0:lockscope><ns0:exclusive /></ns0:lockscope><ns0:depth>infinity</ns0:depth><ns0:owner>.<ns0:href>mailto:xxx.xxxxx@xxxxxx.xxxx</ns0:href>.</ns0:owner><ns0:timeout>Second--1488454203</ns0:timeout><ns0:locktoken><ns0:href>opaquelocktoken:0xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx</ns0:href></ns0:locktoken><ns0:lockroot><ns0:href>/path/file</ns0:href></ns0:lockroot></ns0:activelock></ns0:lockdiscovery></ns0:prop>`

the values should be either positive or -1 which then prevent the client from successfully completing the lock

further more, this number is not corresponding to the max timeout defined value either, which for a lock should be 1week:

LOCK_TIME_OUT_DEFAULT = 604800  # 1 week, in seconds

issue_title: Getting recursive groups using ldap3
issue_body:
I'm working on authentication for a web application and I need to find out the user is a member of a group but I'm having problems getting information between domains in a forest. We basically have the following domain structure

DOMAIN.COM
UK.DOMAIN.COM
US.DOMAIN.COM

All user accounts are in the child domains but the groups are in the parent domain. The groups in the parent domain have groups from the child domains which we are members of.

I can get a list of all groups that I'm a member of and this works well but it only retrieves the memberOf attribute, it doesn't recurs up the tree. I also can't get groups below the parent domain.

Is there an option that I'm missing to allow recursive results? And is traversing between the domains going to be an issue?

issue_title: Feature Request: LayeredBox
issue_body:
When using `Box` to represent a configuration hierarchy, it would be great to be able to have a `LayeredBox` virtually merging multiple `Box`es in layers. That way, `Box`es containing content from say a system wide, user specific and directory specific configuration could be combined while retaining the ability to write back changes using a round-trip yaml parser to preserve formatting and comments in the config provided by the user.

The `LayeredBox` would probably require some flags defining the way merging is done recursively. Should dictionaries at all levels be combined, or should they be overwritten? Should lists be overwritten or merged by appending? Typically, you'd want dictionaries to be merged and lists to be overwritten I think. It's a semantic decision though.

issue_title: Updating default config
issue_body:
I was thinking if we should modify the default configuration in some ways. I'd like to hear your opinions and comments on this subject. I will list the changes that could be made. Please tell if there are more changes you would like to propose.

1. **Hidraw mode as default**
  - The raw / direct bluetooth mode is the default because back in the early days there was no kernel support for the controllers, so it was the only possible mode. However, modern distributions have newer versions of Linux kernel and Bluez, so hidraw works.
  - I think hidraw mode is fundamentally better and with the latest commits and pending pull requests it will support all features in ds4drv. It also supports USB mode.
  - This is breaking change for people that do not specify the hidraw mode in their current configuration. And it might be that hidraw doesn't work for everybody, and some people may prefer the temporary connection to actually pairing the controllers.
  - I will definitely want to everybody's opinion on this one. Do you prefer to use hidraw or raw bluetooth?

2. **Trackpad mouse enabled on controller 1**
  - I think most people would prefer if trackpad mouse was enabled by default on the first controller. It can be disabled in the configuration.

3. **Battery flash by default**
  - Battery flash could be enabled on all controllers by default.

4. **Emulate xpad by default**
  - Most games are made for the Xbox 360 controller and seems that `emulate-xpad` works with most games as intended.
  - However, some games might actually support DualShock 4, and this could break that support.
  - The original DS4 mode could be a default profile in the default configuration.

5. **Mouse sensitivity 0.8**
  - This is just a matter of preference, but I think mouse sensitivity `0.8` is better than `0.6`.

6. **Mouse deadzone 4**
  - The current default mouse deadzone is `5`, and it may be the best value. For my controller I think `4` also works without problems. However, some controllers might have issues with the value `4`. I'd like to hear if `4` works for you, or if the default `5` should be kept.

dnn_face_recognition_ex face descriptor normalization
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a question about the dnn architecture employed in the [dnn_face_recognition_ex.cpp](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/examples/dnn_face_recognition_ex.cpp) example. It looks like the output descriptors are normalized, as the they contain values between -1 and 1.

Sorry if this is kind of a noob question, but I am new to all the deep learning, caffe and tensorflow mess and I am trying to understand the architecture of your net.

The reason I am asking is, I am trying to port the architecture to tensorflow.js, in order to run face-recognition in the browser via WebGL, but I am trying to figure out how to build the output layer.

Thanks in advance for your help, I would highly appreciate your advice.

issue_title: Add option to switch between development and production compiler flags
issue_labels: [{'id': 491283439, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dhermes/bezier/labels/build', 'name': 'build', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False}, {'id': 500859032, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dhermes/bezier/labels/hygiene', 'name': 'hygiene', 'color': '006b75', 'default': False}, {'id': 518253247, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dhermes/bezier/labels/language:%20fortran', 'name': 'language: fortran', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 693077457, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/dhermes/bezier/labels/language:%20python', 'name': 'language: python', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Probably will be easiest to add via env. var. (see c64a97aa5599220b927094a41de04b0c75bbec33, a CLI flag is problematic because `pip` / `setup.py` use the flags for so many other things)

See [dev flags][1]

```
# gfortran
-Wall -Wextra -Wimplicit-interface -fPIC -fmax-errors=1 -g -fcheck=all -fbacktrace
# ifort
-warn all -check all
```

and [prod flags][2]

```
# gfortran
-Wall -Wextra -Wimplicit-interface -fPIC -Werror -fmax-errors=1 -O3 -march=native -ffast-math -funroll-loops
# -warn all -fast
```

on `fortran90.org`.

According to the CircleCI commands [journal][3], we are coming close to production by using the defaults:

```
# YES
-Wall
-fPIC
-O3
-funroll-loops

# NO
-Wextra
-Wimplicit-interface
-Werror
-fmax-errors=1
-march=native
-ffast-math

# ALSO
-ffixed-form  # Just for F77
-g
-fno-second-underscore
```

[1]: http://www.fortran90.org/src/faq.html#what-compiler-options-should-i-use-for-development
[2]: http://www.fortran90.org/src/faq.html#what-compiler-options-should-i-use-for-production-run
[3]: https://github.com/dhermes/bezier/blob/a3a7da9714f27c05037ec171f2655c613a36ede6/.circleci/expected_journal.txt

issue_title: SecretService cleanup
issue_body:
In [this comment](/kang/python-keyring-lib/pull-request/10/add-secret-service-backend#comment-792), Jonathan makes some good suggestions. Let's implement them.

*     Refactor the DBus initialization code to remove duplication.
*     Test a little bit more the supported() method. The existing implementation just checks one 'unsupported' case, and it's not even trivial by reading the test that it should be unsupported
*     Beware of BackendBasicTestCase, you *have* to overload the supported() method otherwise most of the useful tests will be completely skipped!
*     No need to return None in get_password()
*     Document the calls to 'assert prompt == "/"' (comment/assert message) so that we know what it is used for and if it failed, why is it important that it failed.

issue_title: default backend sort order is not deterministic
issue_body:
the default backend is chosen using the following code:


```
#!python

    # if the user doesn't specify a keyring, we apply a default one
    if keyring is None:

        keyrings = backend.get_all_keyring()
        # rank according to the supported result
        keyrings.sort(key = lambda x: -x.supported())
        # get the most recommended one
        keyring = keyrings[0]
```

this isn't deterministic enough because subsequent runs may return a different value if <backend>.supported() is the same value for multiple backends.

for example, on ubuntu, if i run the following a bunch of times:

```
#!python

import keyring
print keyring.get_keyring()
```

sometimes i'll get keyring.backends.Gnome.Keyring and sometimes i'll get keyring.backends.file.PlaintextKeyring.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/96
- Originally reported by: Anonymous
- Originally created at: 2013-03-07T20:26:06.306

issue_title: Rounding of positions
issue_body:
Regarding this [part of the code](https://github.com/pmorissette/bt/blob/master/bt/core.py#L863-866) that calculates how much do we need to buy/sell for rebalancing a position:
```
if amount > 0:
    q = math.floor(amount / (self._price * self.multiplier))
else:
    q = math.ceil(amount / (self._price * self.multiplier))
```
I suggest that we have for both of buys (`amount>0`) and sells (`amound<0`)
```
q = math.floor(amount / (self._price * self.multiplier))
```

Suppose we're holding a portfolio and some securities drop in price, so we need to sell other securities to keep the balance. I think it is better to sell more (`math.floor` for negative amount), so we have positive remainder in the `_capital` and all weights sum to a value less than 1, in contrast to selling less  (`math.ceil` for negative amount) and been overdrafted (negative `_capital`) which would result in a sum of all weights greater than 1.

I understand, that in the present logic fees will overdraft us anyway, but I think that this way of rounding should be still more consistent. In particular if we're working with expensive futures or even equities (this is indeed an extreme, but consider [Lindt & Sprüngli](https://finance.yahoo.com/q;_ylt=ArVeq36GYEDktaKS5l_o9eXxVax_;_ylu=X3oDMTFiNGNzNmlnBHBvcwMxMgRzZWMDeWZpU3ltYm9sTG9va3VwUmVzdWx0cwRzbGsDbHNwbmY-?s=LSPN.F): ~60'000 USD per share). 

Phil, what do you think?

issue_title: Unnormalized triples
issue_body:
Currently all graphs are stored with normalized triples, and the layout engine determines the serialization direction. This is nice, but some groups (namely AMR, who hand-write their graphs) would probably want the directions as-encoded to be retained as much as possible in a round-trip, and also to be able to do operations (e.g. search, manipulation) based on the encoded edge directions. The latter case is also appealing to me, since I could do operations like "sever reentrancies" or "prune subtree" and it would make sense.

A basic solution is to provide a codec that doesn't normalize edge directions (and test that the resulting graph objects are still valid).

issue_title: provider prompt ordering
issue_body:
Re-running `git repo config` has the potential to leak credentials since it seems to randomise which provider it prompts for input for first.

If a lazy user (such as myself) isn't careful and doesn't re-read the prompts on each run due to expecting the order to be the same (as is usually the case for wizards) then it's possible to input the credentials for a provider other than the one you're expecting. The result is that secrets are potentially submitted to the wrong provider which is probably a security concern.

issue_title: Timeouts are not directly configurable
issue_body:
In most of the code, timeouts are handled as number of iterations (which are not always configurable) and depend on the delay factor. Wouldn't it be easier to be able to set the timeouts in terms of seconds (floating)? Here is an example:

```py
        # Check if the only thing you received was a newline
        count = 0
        prompt = prompt.strip()
        while count <= 10 and not prompt:
            prompt = self.read_channel().strip()
            if prompt:
                if self.ansi_escape_codes:
                    prompt = self.strip_ansi_escape_codes(prompt).strip()
            else:
                self.write_channel(self.RETURN)
                time.sleep(delay_factor * .1)
            count += 1
```

Diminishing the `delay_factor` might make the code fail as the timeout is also reduced. I see the delay factor more as a polling interval, it should be configurable without affecting the timeout. It basically means that decreasing it will make the process use more CPU time and nothing else. One could even give a whole CPU to netmiko by setting it 0.

Here is an example of a working replacement:
```py
        # The timeout should be given as an argument
        # it should be configurable globally from the connection handler
        # 11 seconds is the original timeout as the delay_factor defaults to 1, and there are 11 iterations
        timeout = 11
        # Check if the only thing you received was a newline
        prompt = prompt.strip()
        start = time.time()
        while time.time() - start <= timeout and not prompt:
            prompt = self.read_channel().strip()
            if prompt:
                if self.ansi_escape_codes:
                    prompt = self.strip_ansi_escape_codes(prompt).strip()
            else:
                self.write_channel(self.RETURN)
                time.sleep(delay_factor * .1)
```

  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\keyboard\_winkeyboard.py", line 444, in map_char
    scan_code, shift = to_scan_code[name]
TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable
```

**'alt gr,.'** isn't working ,too.

I found out that the configration map used to find scan-codes, does not return a tuple with Shift-Flag:

```python
        from_scan_code[alt_gr_scan_code] = ['alt gr', 'alt gr']
        to_scan_code['alt gr'] = alt_gr_scan_code
    finally:
        tables_lock.release()
```

[see Code](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/master/keyboard/_winkeyboard.py#L355)

The following modification worked for me:
```python
        to_scan_code['alt gr'] = alt_gr_scan_code, False
```

issue_title: TypeError using si_format
issue_body:
I get the the following error when using *si_format* with *Python 3.5.1*:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./x.py", line 8, in <module>
    print("%s s" % (si_format(1/50, precision=2)))
  File "/home/wreiner/coden/python/pwmcalc/si_prefix/__init__.py", line 115, in si_format
    return '%s%s' % (value_str, prefix(expof10))
  File "/home/wreiner/coden/python/pwmcalc/si_prefix/__init__.py", line 67, in prefix
    return prefix[si_level + prefix_levels]
TypeError: string indices must be integers
```

This occurs because the function *prefix* uses floats in

```
return prefix[si_level + prefix_levels]
```

as can be seen when printing the corresponding types:

```
    print("type of si_level: %s" % type(si_level))
    print("type of prefix_levels: %s" % type(prefix_levels)) 
----
type of si_level: <class 'float'>
type of prefix_levels: <class 'float'>
```

To **fix** this issue both variables should be casted to int:

```
return prefix[(int)(si_level) + (int)(prefix_levels)]
```

issue_title: Emacs - Jedi not recognising dist-packages libraries
issue_body:
I installed jedi on my emacs 24+ and goto-defintion works well for within a file/buffer. But for libraries sitting in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages (or) for packages dynamically added to sys.path, it just dumps Definition not found.

I never use virtualenv for my python development and hence, all my third party python lib files are lying in one central location. How to force emacs-jedi to use them (or) in worst case, does jedi need virtualenv setup always?
Note - mine is a ubuntu 14.04 machine.

issue_title: [question] Custom parameter type inference
issue_body:
I see that Jedi can perform parameter type inference on docstrings and pep484, however I'm trying to add my own (for quite a custom use-case).

The use-case is that functions are wrapped in decorators, and based on the decorator we know the types of all parameters that will be passed to the function. Therefore I'd like to tell Jedi about these without requiring the types to be declared in docstrings. 

I've taken a stab at this by patching the `jedi.evaluate.param.ExecutedParam.infer` method and calling out to an additional source of hints. However I'm struggling to understand what "ContextSets" are and how to go about using them.

I'm able to correctly infer the fully qualified name of the type for each parameter, I just don't know how to turn the string "my.Type" into something Jedi is happy with.

issue_title: [iproute2 rules] Cannot specified a fwmark with mask
issue_body:
### Observation

If you use the `ip rule` command manually, you can create masked firewall marks:

```sh
ip rule add pref 1000 fwmark 0x100/0x101 lookup mytable
```

```python
from pyroute2.iproute import IPRoute

ip = IPRoute()

fwmark = 0x100
fwmask = 0x101

# Adding as a concat string separated by a slash?
# Nope, the .rule() method will not parse it and an exception is thrown
ip.rule("add",
    priority=1000,
    table=1,
    fwmark="/".join([hex(fwmark), hex(fwmask)]))


# Adding by direct attr injection into method?
# Also no, the .rule() method does not check kwargs for their presence and there
# is no differentiation in the resulting rules from calling with the same arguments
# minus FRA_FWMARK and FRA_FWMASK
ip.rule("add",
    priority=1000,
    table=1,
    FRA_FWMARK=fwmark,
    FRA_FWMASK=fwmask)
```

### Proposition
I think the latter example would probably work out better in that there's less code to be added, less tests, and lower-level control with the addition of a few `if` blocks in [pyroute2.iproute.IPRoute.rule()](../blob/84f99fbbce049517300a08c0389bf27765e1e4f6/pyroute2/iproute.py#L1099).

issue_title: attachment://example_file.png fails on rewrite
issue_body:
There is a problem with using the `_` character when specifying an image attachment as an embed image. This is reproducible via the following example

This works:
```
e = discord.Embed().set_image(url='attachment://examplefile.png')
await ctx.send(embed=e, file=discord.File('test.png', 'examplefile.png'))
```
This does not work:
```
e = discord.Embed().set_image(url='attachment://example_file.png')
await ctx.send(embed=e, file=discord.File('test.png', 'example_file.png'))
```

issue_title: unoconv includes deleted text when converting from doc to txt
issue_body:
Hi!

When a `.doc` file with recorded changes is converted to a `.txt`, deleted text appears in the converted version. The same happens when using LibreOffice directly but a work around is to disable the "Show changes" option in LibreOffice menu. There is no such option with unoconv.

(This bug was originally reported to Debian BTS as bug [#624295](http://bugs.debian.org/624295))

I've also dockerized this script so that I can run it as a Job in kubernetes: https://hub.docker.com/r/jar349/kafka-test/

When I run Kafka in docker-compose and then run my script on the host, everything works.
When I run Kafka in kubernetes (minikube) and then my docker image as a Job, everything works.

If I try to link my docker container to the docker-compose cluster, the producer times out getting metadata
If I try to run my script against the exposed kafka service in kubernetes, the producer times out getting metadata

```
docker run --rm -a STDOUT -a STDERR --network="kafkadocker_default" --link kafkadocker_kafka_1 -e "KAFKA_HOST=kafkadocker_kafka_1" -e "KAFKA_PORT=9092" jar349/kafka-test:latest

    metadata = future_metadata.get(timeout=10)
  File "/opt/test/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/kafka/producer/future.py", line 62, in get
    "Timeout after waiting for %s secs." % timeout)
kafka.errors.KafkaTimeoutError: KafkaTimeoutError: Timeout after waiting for 10 secs.

Here's my docker-compose.yml:
```
version: '2'
services:
  zookeeper:
    image: wurstmeister/zookeeper
    ports:
      - "2181:2181"
  kafka:
    image: wurstmeister/kafka:0.11.0.1

Here's my kubernetes Job:
```
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: kafka-test
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: kafka-test
        image: jar349/kafka-test:latest
        env:
        - name: KAFKA_HOST
          value: "kafka-kafka"
        - name: KAFKA_PORT
          value: "9092"
      restartPolicy: Never
  backoffLimit: 4
```
Naturally, my first thought was that something was wrong with my networking.  But in each failure case, I was able to telnet to the host on port 9092 and connect to KAFKA (I saw the error log in the kafka server).

So in the case of not connecting to docker-compose from my docker container, I ran:
```
docker run --rm -it --network="kafkadocker_default" --link "kafkadocker_kafka_1:kafka" -e "KAFKA_HOST=kafka" -e "KAFKA_PORT=9092" jar349/kafka-test:latest /bin/bash
```
and inside of the docker container, I can ping kafka and also telnet to kafka on 9092:
```
# ping -c 2 kafka
PING kafka (172.20.0.3): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 172.20.0.3: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.137 ms
64 bytes from 172.20.0.3: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.153 ms

2 packets transmitted, 2 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max/stddev = 0.137/0.145/0.153/0.000 ms

# telnet kafka 9092
Trying 172.20.0.3...
Connected to kafka.
Escape character is '^]'.
HELLO
Connection closed by foreign host.

--- and in the kafka server log ---

WARN Unexpected error from /172.20.0.4; closing connection (org.apache.kafka.common.network.Selector)
org.apache.kafka.common.network.InvalidReceiveException: Invalid receive (size = 1212501068 larger than 104857600)
```

In the case where I try to run my script against an exposed kafka service, I can telnet there too but my script gets the timeout error shown above.

issue_title: Add additional values to API_VERSIONS for 0.10.x
issue_body:
Currently [`API_VERSIONS`](https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/client_async.py#L78)only has one value for 0.10 brokers. 

For safety, I'm trying to pin the Kafka broker API versions in our code. 
If there's a future 0.10.x release that breaks an API, there's no way I can demarcate that our code wasn't expecting anything newer than 0.10.0 API or 0.10.1 API version... 

Should we add values for those?

Also, It seems tedious to be adding values every time a new Kafka release happens. Is there something I'm missing? Like can I specify `(0, 10, 0)` in my code? And if an api changes, kafka-python will understand that that means 0.10.0.x?

issue_title: Coverage combine is additive.
issue_body:
Discussed at PyCon 2016.

When running `coverage combine`, coverage will merge the information not only from every generated covfile, but also from the `.coverage` result file from the previous run of `coverage combine` if that file is still in the directory.

That can lead to misleading coverage results where lines are reported as covered because they *were* covered at some time in the past, even if they aren't now.

issue_title: New "Ports scanner" plugin
issue_body:
The "ports scanner" plugin aims at monitoring local or remote TCP/ICMP ports status. A list of remote host/port is define in the configuration file and the result is displayed in the UI.

Configuration file example:

    [ports]
    port_1_description=Internet connection
    port_1_hostport=www.google.fr:80
    port_2_description=Ping my router
    port_2_hostport=192.168.0.254

Note: if no port is provided, use ICMP instead of TCP.

UI:

    Internet connection    Port unreachable (red)
    Ping my router         10ms (green)

Source:

* https://docs.python.org/2/library/asyncore.html#module-asyncore
* http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1205863/how-can-i-get-non-blocking-socket-connects

For ICMP: all need **root** right !!!
* For ICMP: https://github.com/volftomas/pyicmp/blob/master/ping.py
* For ICMP (bis): https://github.com/samuel/python-ping/blob/master/ping.py
* For ICMP (ter): https://gist.github.com/pklaus/856268
* For ICMP (last but not least): https://bitbucket.org/delroth/python-ping/src/7084c31fa1603a1071c30525ae0431da42ce93cd/ping.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default

Also have a look on http://denilson.sa.nom.br/prettyping/ (for the nice graph idea)

issue_title: Target hosts via docker connection
issue_body:
We use ansible to provision our systems.  Some of those systems are bare metal, others are vagrant vms, and others are docker containers.

### Ansible Connection

We currently test our baremetal and vagrant VMs via the ansible connection.  Tests run against all hosts unless the test contains a `testinfra_hosts` list.  This list can contain hostnames or ansible groups.  We happen to target the [ansible group](https://github.com/metacloud/molecule/blob/master/tests/functional/vagrant/scenarios/full/tests/test_example-group1.py).

The command executed is:

` $ testinfra tests/ --connection=ansible --ansible-inventory=.molecule/ansible_inventory`

### Docker Connection

We use the docker connection, and pass all of the hosts on the command line.  So tests which should run on host1 run across host1 and host2.  We cannot leave `--hosts` off in lieu of `--ansible-inventory`.  Since Ansible 2.x supports a docker connection, could we not use the ansible backend so long as `--ansible-inventory` is specifying the connection as `docker`?

The command executed is:

`$ testinfra tests/ --connection=docker --hosts=full-01,full-02'`

### Reproduce

Checkout [our repo](https://github.com/metacloud/molecule)

```
$ pip install molecule
$ cd tests/functional/docker/scenarios/full
# Remove the `pytestmark` mark from the tests `tests/functional/docker/scenarios/full/tests/test_example*.py`
$ molecule test # docker must be installed
```

issue_title: Resource.meta should be immutable
issue_body:
There is a bug here https://github.com/klen/flask-restler/blob/develop/flask_restler/mongo.py#L228
```python
        if self.meta.aggregate:
            self.meta.aggregate = [p for p in self.meta.aggregate if '$sort' not in p]
            self.meta.aggregate.append({'$sort': sorting})
```

self.meta is a class field thus it's value shared among all instances of the `Resource` class.  

Proposal:
1. Make `self.meta` immutable
2. Add `self.aggregate` field to the `MongoResource` class and copy `self.meta.aggregate` value to it on instantiation.

Example pseudo-code:
```python
class  MongoResource(Resource):
    def __init__(self, collection):
        self.aggregate = self.meta.aggregete.copy()
```

issue_title: pyodbc reads random characters in column name when description is set for this column
Add native support for Python UUID data type
issue_body:
Looking through the Wiki documentation on [Data Types](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Data-Types/de8c706abd83214d69e6be0b5c2bb58b2c916212), I noticed that the SQL_GUID type is being converted to/from a string, instead of using [the native `UUID` type](https://docs.python.org/2/library/uuid.html) provided by Python. The SQL_GUID type applies to the `uniqueidentifer` column type, which [pyodbc appears to be testing](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/blob/f94251a5b62252fff0e7057b6f70260cb4bf4fb0/tests2/accesstests.py#L426-L435) against.

These tests don't appear to have changed since 2008 (added in https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/commit/c3f6b46d8a4f73722439b4755ef5df42adff81d9), and there is a comment for the test that alludes to Python not having a UUID type. This was probably true back in 2008, but Python has come a long way since then and now it does have a UUID type.

issue_title: Support interface normalized to [-1,1) range?
issue_body:
I would like to interface Numpy arrays to 16, 24, 32 and 64-bit WAV formats, and PySoundFile looks like a good match for my needs.  I saw the discussion in issue #17, and I plan to wait until the next version that avoids truncating 64-bit data to 32-bit before I install PySoundFile.

I plan to do all my signal processing with dtype=float64 and a range of -1 to +1.  For 32-bit and 64-bit floating-point WAV files, this is the natural range.  For the PCM WAV formats, scaling will be necessary to achieve this in the interface to Numpy arrays.  I found another Python WAV interface package that supports this style:
https://github.com/gesellkammer/sndfileio
However, it does not support the 64-bit WAV format or Python 3.

My suggestion for PySoundFile is to support the [-1,1) range for audio data.  I am not clear if PySoundFile already does this when using float64 Numpy arrays with PCM WAV files.  If not, my suggestion is to add this as an option.  Maybe using an optional 'normalized' argument.  This would avoid the need for me to provide wrapper functions around PySoundFile's read and write functions in order to do the scaling that depends on the number of bits in the PCM format.

The scaling could be implemented in Numpy with the ldexp() function.  I have not programmed in C++, but I think I saw that the ldexp() function in C++ also, which would be ideal for speed.

issue_title: Refactor Cython package hierarchy
issue_body:
Steps:
* Refactor .pyx files to get rid of the `include "cefpython.pyx"` (and all other include statements). Each pyx file should be a real independent module with its own imports.
* Refactor setup.py and move to tools/cython/
  * Worth reading:
    * How To Create A Hierarchy Of Modules In A Package: https://github.com/cython/cython/wiki/PackageHierarchy
    * cythonize(["*.pyx"]): https://github.com/cython/cython/wiki/enhancements-distutils_preprocessing
    * For more see [Cython wiki](https://github.com/cython/cython/wiki)
  * Use modular structure - get rid of fix_pyx_files.py script. To keep backward compatibility create \__init\__.py with all the imports from sub-modules.
* Search for binaries/libraries in build/cef_binary_linux64/ (bin/ lib/) instead of current src/linux/binaries_64bit/ and src/linux/setup/lib_64bit/.
* Refactor make-setup.py and move to tools/
* Refactor compile.py and move to tools/build.py

Directories:
* src/*.pyx
* utils/*.pyx - move utils.pyx, string_utils.pyx, window_utils_*.pyx and others
* handlers/*.pyx - done
* extern/*.pxd - done
* extern/cef/*.pxd - done
* extern/views/*.pxd (CEF views)

This issue is blocking other features that are to be implemented:
* CEF views - new classes should be imported from "cef.views" package (Issue #252)
* Automatically import enum types with translator.py tool and put them in "cef.types" package (Issue #214)

Related issue: #208 (Big changes planned for CEF Python).

issue_title: Multiple subparsers leads to recursive variables
issue_body:
```
#!/usr/bin/python
# PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK
import argparse
import argcomplete
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest='command')
parser1 = subparsers.add_parser('local')
parser1.add_argument('name', choices=['zzz', 'aaa'])
parser2 = subparsers.add_parser('lookup')
parser2.add_argument('dest', choices=['a', 'b', 'c'])
sub2 = parser2.add_subparsers(dest='type')
parser3 = sub2.add_parser('env')
parser3.add_argument('name', choices=['zzz', 'aaa'])
parser4 = sub2.add_parser('etc')
parser4.add_argument('file', choices=['my.cnf', 'apache.conf'])

argcomplete.autocomplete(parser)
data = parser.parse_args()
```

If you go one subparser deep, into the local branch, this works as expected. Once you fill out zzz or aaa, you're no longer offered autocomplete choices.

```
$ ./testcase.py local zzz [tab][tab]
$
```

If you go into the lookup branch and hit the second subparser, the subparser choice is presented to you as many variables deep as you care to go.

e.g.

```
$ ./testcase.py lookup env env env [tab][tab]
env     etc     -h      --help 
$
```

Interestingly, if a non subparser argument is added before the inital one, you get offered the correct choices mostly (when you hit a,b,c you get env and etc in the options as well, if you pick a, b, or c completion works properly from then on, but if you choose etc or env, it goes back into looping forever).

issue_title: remote tempdir only works on Linux-like systems
issue_body:
`BaseRemoteMachine.tempdir` runs `mktemp -d`, with no other parameters.

With the Todd Miller or GNU coreutils versions of `mktemp`, this works fine (at least under GNU, it uses a template of `tmp.XXXXXXXXXX`).

But with most other implementations, it's an error. The traditional command (it's not actually standardized, but was added in OpenBSD and FreeBSD and copied from there to many other systems) requires a template parameter. Most current *BSD versions allow a prefix instead of a template, but you still have to specify one or the other.

This means that if you try to use `tempdir` on a `RemoteMachine` that's connected to, say, OS X, or FreeBSD, you get a `ProcessExecutionError`.

This also means that RPyC ZeroDeploy doesn't work on OS X, FreeBSD, etc. targets.

Changing the command to explicitly use `tmp.XXXXXXXXXX` will make it work _almost_ everywhere, and work exactly the same as it has on almost every linux distro (and on Cygwin), which I think is the best option.

issue_title: Single source package version
issue_body: Currently `setup.py` loads `__version__` from `vulture/__init__.py` which in turn loads it form `vulture/main.py` - this is pretty dangerous  because there is no guarantee that all the dependencies of the package would have been installed and loaded when version is being loaded for. https://packaging.python.org/guides/single-sourcing-package-version/ mentions quite a few ways of dealing with this.

issue_title: Lock with leases
issue_body:
I was browsing through this client while working on a [version for node](https://github.com/WatchBeam/etcd3). I noticed that your locking is implemented on a straight k/v without using a lease. I suggest you consider using a lease for this, so that if something happens to the server or application which makes the lock, the resource isn't abandoned indefinitely. The downside is that it's possible that a lock is 'lost' while a process thinks it has it, but the set of situations in which that can occur and cause issues is a strict subset of the situations (only one: a partial network partition) in which the lock would otherwise be held indefinitely.

My implementation and little more reasoning is here, if you're interested: https://github.com/WatchBeam/etcd3/pull/5

issue_title: clang2py can't handle incomplete structures.
issue_body:
clang2py doesn't generate code for incomplete C structures.

Considering next header file:
```
#ifndef T_H
#define T_H

struct Foo;
void do_something(struct Foo* foo);

#endif
```

clang2py issues a few warnings and doesn't generate definitions for ``struct Foo`` and ```do_something```

```
clang2py -o t.py t.h 
ERROR:cursorhandler:Structure struct_Foo is Incomplete t.h:5 align:-2 size:-2
ERROR:cursorhandler:Structure struct_Foo is Incomplete t.h:5 align:-2 size:-2
```

Generated t.py is empty:
```
cat t.py 
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
#
# TARGET arch is: []
# WORD_SIZE is: 8
# POINTER_SIZE is: 8
# LONGDOUBLE_SIZE is: 16
#
import ctypes




__all__ = \
    []

```

Having declared, but not defined structures is most common way in C to hide library internals and it's widely used.

issue_title: Importing envtpl 0.5.2 breaks stdout and stderr on python3
issue_body:
envtpl module wraps sys.stdout and sys.stderr with streamwriter which outputs bytes.
And in python3.5.1 sys.stdout and sys.stderr are in text mode by default and raise TypeError if you try to write() bytes in it.
See https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/sys.html#sys.stderr

issue_title: Wrong events when moving files to non-watched directory
issue_body:
When moving files from a watched directory to a non-watched directory on Linux (Ubuntu 14.04, Kernel 3.13) I get deletion events instead of move events. Here is a small test script:

    #!/usr/bin/env python

    import errno
    import logging
    import os
    import os.path
    import sys
    import time

    import watchdog.version
    from watchdog.observers import *
    from watchdog.events import *

    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(message)s')

    logging.info("Python version is %r" % (sys.version_info[:3],))
    logging.info("Watchdog version is %r" % (watchdog.version.__version__,))

    def create_file(path):
        with open(path, 'w') as f:
            f.write('...')

    # Prepare files
    os.mkdir('source')
    os.mkdir('target')
    os.mkdir('source/bar')
    create_file('source/foo')
    create_file('source/bar/baz')

    observer = Observer()
    event_handler = LoggingEventHandler()
    observer.schedule(event_handler, 'source', recursive=True)
    observer.start()

    os.rename('source/foo', 'source/foo2')
    os.rename('source/bar', 'source/bar2')
    os.rename('source/foo2', 'target/foo3')
    os.rename('source/bar2', 'target/bar3')

    time.sleep(2)  # Wait until events have been processed
    observer.stop()
    observer.join()

The output I get:

    Python version is (2, 7, 6)
    Watchdog version is (0, 8, 2)
    Moved file: from source/foo to source/foo2
    Modified directory: source
    Moved directory: from source/bar to source/bar2
    Modified directory: source
    Deleted file: source/foo2
    Modified directory: source
    Deleted directory: source/bar2
    Modified directory: source

As you can see, the moves inside the watched directory generate the right events. However, the moves to a non-watched directory only generate deletion events.

issue_title: QueueManager has memory consistency issues
issue_body:
The last few days, I have been experimenting with making a Plex Music Player for Alexa.
However, I have run into some issues regarding Python and shared memory access.
I have tried both the Queue and collections.deque methods to build a QueueManager with, as displayed in your example _samples/audio/playlist_demo_
However, with both solutions, I run into the problem that global memory access and Flask doesn’t correspond well together.
Most of the times, it is possible to access the information provided in the queue.
For example, I have written a method that allows Alexa to describe the song being played.
However, this is not always the current song being played. Sometimes, it responds with a previously played song or _None_. As these are previous values of self._current, the problem probably lies in the fact that there is no coherency between memory here.
 
Have you perhaps also experienced this issue, and if so, is there a viable solution to working with shared memory for these values? Following Python documentation, _collections.deque_ should provide thread-safe implementations for both pop* and append*. However, when I’m requesting the lengths of the current queue’s for example, they sometimes return as 0, which relates to the same problem.

issue_title: MissingFieldError: expires_date_ms during validation on Server Notification reception
issue_body:
Important informations to reproduce:
- itunes-iap==2.5.0 (latest available on pip)
- using an auto-renewable subscription, with a server receiving Status Update Notifications (feature was added by Apple during July 2017)
- the issue does NOT happen when first purchasing, but when the renewal happens and your server is called. For practical purpose, you only need to intercept the 'latest_receipt' or 'latest_expired_receipt' (whichever is present) part of the POSTed JSON and try to verify it manually.

I am at a loss if this issue is poor documentation by Apple or an actual bug with Apple API, but when trying to verify a receipt with those conditions, itunes-iap will throw "MissingFieldError: expires_date_ms". Neverthless, it would be great if itunes-iap could handle this gracefully.

Please find attached an anonymised status notification call and anonymised response of the API which exhibit the issue when trying to validate after that status notification.

You'll notice that the Status Notification doesn't have expires_date_ms either, so if itunes-iap want to support it at some point, it'll need to be able to work without that field.

[apple-status-notification.txt](https://github.com/youknowone/itunes-iap/files/1337759/apple-status-notification.txt)

issue_title: Why is "allow_headers" now a case-sensitive match?
issue_body:
With version 3.x the list of headers returned by `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` is now a case-sensitive match against the list of headers defined in the `CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS` setting.

For example, with version 2.1.3 if the setting for "allow_headers" was ...
```
CORS_ALLOW_HEADERS = ['Content-Type', 'Authorization']
```
... then the following works ...

```
$ http OPTIONS :5000 Origin:http://localhost:5001 Access-Control-Request-Headers:'authorization, Content-type' Access-Control-Request-Method:GET
OPTIONS / HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization, Content-type
Access-Control-Request-Method: GET
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 0
Host: localhost:5000
Origin: http://localhost:5001
User-Agent: HTTPie/0.9.3

HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-type, authorization
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: DELETE, GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, PATCH, POST, PUT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:5001
Allow: HEAD, GET, POST, OPTIONS
Content-Length: 0
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Date: Tue, 06 Sep 2016 13:36:22 GMT
Server: Werkzeug/0.11.11 Python/2.7.9
```

However, with version 3.x it does not return the `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` header because there was no **case-sensitive** match.

```
$ http OPTIONS :5000 Origin:http://localhost:5001 Access-Control-Request-Headers:'authorization, Content-type' Access-Control-Request-Method:GET
OPTIONS / HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization, Content-type
Access-Control-Request-Method: GET
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 0
Host: localhost:5000
Origin: http://localhost:5001
User-Agent: HTTPie/0.9.3

HTTP/1.0 200 OK
Access-Control-Allow-Methods: DELETE, GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, PATCH, POST, PUT
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:5001
Allow: HEAD, GET, POST, OPTIONS
Content-Length: 0
Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8
Date: Tue, 06 Sep 2016 13:39:34 GMT
Server: Werkzeug/0.11.11 Python/2.7.9
```

This breaks CORS on most browsers including Chrome and Firefox. However, not on IE apparently.

issue_title: Module-level functions or instance methods for helpers
issue_body:
There is a need to have a number of helper functions, such as asdict(), astuple(), replace(), etc. This issue is not about which specific functions we need (that will come later), but rather how to make these functions available.

namedtuple handles this by having member functions that begin with an underscore, such as ._make(), ._asdict(), ._replace(), ._fields(), etc.

attrs handles this by using module-level functions that take an instance as a parameter, such as .attrs.fields(), attrs.has(), attrs.asdict(), etc.

I'm leaning towards the attrs interface of using module-level functions. I don't like the underscores required by namedtuple's approach, and even then you could have name collisions.

issue_title: OAuth2 client: send credentials via HTTP basic auth
issue_body:
According to [RFC 6749, Section 2.3](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-2.3):

> The authorization server MUST support the HTTP Basic authentication scheme for authenticating clients that were issued a client password.
>  
> For example (with extra line breaks for display purposes only):
> Authorization: Basic czZCaGRSa3F0Mzo3RmpmcDBaQnIxS3REUmJuZlZkbUl3
>
> Alternatively, the authorization server MAY support including the client credentials in the request-body...

The `remote_app` flow for `authorized_response` in the method `handle_oauth2_response` (class: `OAuthRemoteApp`, file: `client.py`) **only** sends the credentials in the request-body via `client_secret` and `client_id` params. Therefore, the client doesn't work for servers that don't support request-body credentials, what is acceptable from the server, since the RFC states that the server MAY support request-body, but MUST support HTTP basic auth. 

There should be a switch flag and by default use HTTP basic auth.

issue_title: Possible issue with ampersand and underscore encoding
issue_body:
Hi!

Tracing down a bug in a [project of mine](https://github.com/Nagasaki45/bibo), that is based on [pybtex](https://pybtex.org/) (that uses `latexcodec`) lead me to think that there might be a problem with encoding of ampersands and underscores in `latexcodec`. The examples bellow are for ampersands but the same apply for underscores.

I have a `.bib` file like this:

```
@ARTICLE{johndoe,
    author = "Doe, John",
    title = "hello \& world",
    year = "2020",
}
```
The problem is that I read the file using pybtex, and then write it back to file, and get:

```
@ARTICLE{johndoe,
    author = "Doe, John",
    title = "hello \\& world",
    year = "2020",
}
```

Notice the extra backslash.

It seems that pybtex doesn't really manipulate the value `"hello \& world"` when it reads the file. But it uses `latexcodec` encoding to write it back to file. Using `latexcodec` directly I get the following:

```
>>> import latexcodec
>>> codecs.encode('hello \\& world', 'ulatex+UTF-8')
'hello \\\\& world'

>>> print(codecs.encode('hello \\& world', 'ulatex+UTF-8'))
hello \\& world
```

I'm not 100% sure that you considered it as a bug, because I don't know the `.bib` specs (if such thing even exists). I also noticed that there is some kind of handling of ampersands in `latexcodec`.

If it is a bug I will be happy to help solving it.

Thanks!

import tornado.web
import tornado.ioloop
import tornado.gen
import tornado.options
import tornado.httpserver

class MainHandler(tornado.web.RequestHandler):

    executor = ProcessPoolExecutor(1)

    @tornado.gen.coroutine

        num = int(self.request.body)
        result = yield self.executor.submit(pow, num, 2)

application = tornado.web.Application([

])

issue_title: Use comments to parse mediawiki file
issue_body:
Hi

I have media wiki files with the following structure:

```
<!-- Begin Titel -->

some content

<!-- End Titel -->

<!-- Begin Metadaten -->

some content

<!-- End Metadaten -->

and so on ...
```

These comments express the structure of the file. So I think the method `filter_comments` is convenient to get all the "comments" as a list:

```

['<!-- Begin Titel -->', '<!-- End Titel -->', '<!-- Begin Bilder -->', '<!-- End Bilder -->', '<!-- Begin Metadaten -->', '<!-- End Metadaten -->', '<!-- Begin Transkription -->', '<!-- End Transkription -->', '<!-- Begin Referenzen -->', '<!-- End Referenzen -->', '<!-- Begin Kategorien -->', '<!-- End Kategorien -->']


```

I can get another list of all the elements of the file by accessing `nodes`.

In this list, I can now search for all the elements contained in "comments" to get the indexes of their actual content elements in `nodes`.

So the script looks like this:

```python

#!/usr/bin/env python3

import mwparserfromhell

file = open("mediawikifile", "r")
wikicode = file.read()

parsed = mwparserfromhell.parse(wikicode)

# all the comments in the file
comments = parsed.filter_comments()

# all the elements of the file (segmented according to unknown rules, at least unknown to me)
nodes = parsed.nodes

# get the contents of the first comment
begin_1 = nodes.index(comments[0])
begin_2 = nodes.index(comments[1])
contents = nodes[begin_1+1:begin_2] # get all the elements between the start and the end of the first comment

 


```

Is this there a better way to do that?

issue_title: Tokens issue
issue_body:
@mcaceresb

The specific issue here is that strings are tokenized differently than text. With my current code joining method, I was thinking there would be two different Token types, `Token.Text` and `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`, where the latter is a contiguous set of tokens that comprise a block (and which thus must be sent all at once). 

Obviously I forgot that I had also defined the token type `Token.Literal.String`. Since this is a different name than the earlier two token types, the loop parsed it into a different line.

I need to leave the string token type so that it catches comments, and also within blocks:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15164633/43841838-0d476da8-9af2-11e8-81fa-083f653ae1e1.png)


This is the part where it's superbly annoying that there isn't a way with Pygments to show the entire token stack. It only shows the topmost token, so I can't see (I don't think) that above the `Token.Literal.String.Double` is inside a `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`. 

I think the main way to solve this is to set the token name for strings that occur within a block to `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`

Submitting a request - is it possible that django-social-auth could add support to force re-authentication with Facebook when the consuming application's session is not valid (or through an API call)?

Details:
Our application (for security reasons) requires the user to re-authenticate more frequently than Facebook defaults. In addition, there are times where we would want someone to re-authenticate regardless if they have an active Facebook login.

Can django-social-auth provide the ability to force re-authentication or expiration of the Facebook sessions?

issue_title: Clean up the 'magic' - Let Developer decide what to do.
issue_body:
Current implementation tries to impose to many things on developer, and does to much "magic". Social-Auth assumes:

1. social-auth should create a user account, and fetch some data from provider X to user model.
2. social-auth should not create a user.
The decision is based on SOCIAL_AUTH_CREATE_USERS.

IMHO, this approach is wrong. It suggests one of 2 mentioned ways, but there is little space for developer to define what he want's to do ( Developer can not control the way users are created. )

I think, creating user account etc, should be left to developer by default. Django-social-auth, could have built in ("generic") for creating account (with current funcionality), but usage of SOCIAL_AUTH_CREATE_USERS flag, should be deprecated (this looks like "magic")

Instead of using the flag, developer could do:

```
from social_auth.signals import auth_ok, create_user # or other name
auth_ok.connect(create_user) # or something else - custom way of creating users
```
Developer will be able to control the way users are created or use "generic user registration", if he doesn't need so much controll.


Example, why is this approach better:
Currently, when User uses multiple backends (providers), and has different first name and second name in those backends, everytime User shwitches backends, django-social-auth changes User model, according to extra_data from backend.

issue_title: Django Migrations Broken
issue_body:
#916 changed the app name from "default" to "social_auth". Users running the app prior this change will have migrations registered under the old name, resulting in Django thinking it should apply migrations from scratch.

These existing migrations should be faked with the following command:

```bash
python manage.py migrate social_auth 0004 --fake
```

issue_body:
I have a unique constraint on a couple of fields of a model. I can see the "Recover deleted" list but when I click on a deleted object that - if it were to be recovered - would violate the unique constraint, then safe_revert() throws RevertError("Could not revert revision, due to database integrity errors.").

issue_title: Bug with format and for-loop
issue_body:
I have spotted that this example gives me false negative.
No errors is created, but there should be one.

```python
def validate_date(date: str) -> datetime:
    """
    Validates that value is a valid date.

    >>> validate_date('20.12.2010')
    datetime.datetime(2010, 12, 20, 0, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>)

    """
    valid_formats = [
        '%d.%m.%y',
        '%d.%m.%Y',
        '%e.%m.%y',
        '%e.%m.%Y',
    ]

    for format in valid_formats:  # format is built-in name
        try:
            return datetime.strptime(date, format).replace(
                tzinfo=timezone(settings.TIME_ZONE),
            )
        except ValueError:
            continue
```

`format` is a built-in name that is not checked for some reason.

issue_title: Add a new config to include some specific 2.0 endpoints
issue_body:
Currently, incremental migration of endpoints from v1.2 to v2.0 is not possible. If `swagger_versions:['1.2','2.0']` is present in the config and `swagger.json` is present (which is very small right now and contains only a single migrated endpoint), then `pyramid_swagger` will look for all other endpoints in the same `swagger.json` and hence fail.

The proposed solution is to add a new config :  
```
pyramid_swagger.include_2dot0_routes = ['endpoint_route1']
```
And IF `2.0` is in `swagger_versions` AND `include_2dot0_routes` is non-empty AND current route is in `include_2dot0_routes`, only then go for `2.0` swagger_handler otherwise use the `1.2` swagger handler.

issue_title: rendervariations doesn't use custom render_variations
issue_body:
Hello!

When rerender variations through ```python manage.py rendervariations 'app_name.model_name.field_name' --replace``` stdimage use built-in ```render_variation``` function skipping ```render_variations``` parameter from ```StdImageField```.

How to reproduce:
1. Create the field in the model with custom render_variations function.
2. Create model instance.
3. Check result variations.
4. Run ```python manage.py rendervariations 'app_name.model_name.field_name' --replace```

issue_title: How important is Python 2.7 and 3.0-3.3 support for everyone using this package?
issue_labels: [{'id': 1001306955, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMDAxMzA2OTU1', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/frejanordsiek/hdf5storage/labels/Community%20Question', 'name': 'Community Question', 'color': '2d5a93', 'default': False}, {'id': 442166519, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw0NDIxNjY1MTk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/frejanordsiek/hdf5storage/labels/Python%20version%20support%20drop', 'name': 'Python version support drop', 'color': '5319e7', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I've been steadily working towards the first feature 1.0 release (currently labelled as 0.2 in master to denote that it isn't ready yet) and will hopefully release it within the next year.

A major question is what versions of Python to support. Python 2.6 support was already dropped in the master branch.

I bring this up because simultaneously supporting Python 2.7 and 3.x makes for a complicated codebase due having to handle the unicode vs. bytes issue properly; so removing Python 2.7 support would allow the code to become significantly smaller and cleaner and thus easier to maintain. Dropping Python 3.0, 3.1, 3.2, and/or 3.3 support would allow major cleanups and improvements in the unit tests. Additionally, I am no longer able to test 3.0 and 3.1 on travis, so it is hard to detect regressions.

By the time this package has its 1.0 release, will anybody be using it on Python 2.7, 3.0, 3.1, 3.2, and/or 3.3 or know someone else who is?

If people need continuing support for these python versions, then version 1.0.x (1.0 and bugfixes) will support them. However, support for these versions will definitely be dropped by the 2.0 release (if there ever is one), and possibly the 1.1 release if no one is using them by then.

issue_title: Preventing library from being added by hook
issue_body:
Thanks for the package, I'm finding it really useful.

I'm currently trying to use this when building a library, `django-verified-user`, which defines the `verified_user` namespace.

When I run `seed-isort-config` from the command line, it detects that the library has been installed using `./setup develop` and prevents `verified_user` from being added to the list of `known_third_party`.

However, using `seed-isort-config` as a `pre-commit` hook runs the tool in a separate virtual environment, meaning it doesn't detect the local installation and adds `verified_user` to `known_third_party`.

I'd like some way to prevent `seed-isort-config` from adding `verified_user` to `known_third_party` when it's run as a hook.

One possible solution is similar to #10: detect that `verified_user` is specified in `known_first_party` to ensure it's never added to `known_third_party`.

Another would be to add an `exclude-pkg` (`excludepkg`? `exclude-dep`?) flag to the command, allowing me to simply specify `args: [--exclude-pkg=verified_user,not_included_either]` in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`

Happy to put together a PR if one of these sounds more desirable than the other. I'm leaning towards the second option due to implementation simplicity, but defer to you, @asottile.

issue_title: python3 and dump
issue_body:
There's a problem and/or misleading documentation where `frontmatter.dump` is concerned.  I figured I'd give a full example, but the easy fix for you is to just say

> Use `BytesIO` with `frontmatter.dump` if you are using Python3.

So I'm using `frontmatter` to parse and re-write some things.  It goes something like this

```console
$ python3
Python 3.6.1 (default, May  8 2017, 14:55:30) 
[GCC 5.3.1 20160406 (Red Hat 5.3.1-6)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import frontmatter
>>> from io import StringIO, BytesIO
>>> with open("awkward.json", "r") as f:
...     meta, content = frontmatter.parse(f.read())
... 
>>> str_buff = StringIO()
>>> bytes_buff = BytesIO()
>>> 
>>> # ...made changes to meta and/or content ...
... 
>>> post = frontmatter.Post(content, handler=None, **meta)
>>> 
>>> frontmatter.dump(post, str_buff)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/opt/spack/opt/spack/linux-fedora23-x86_64/gcc-5.3.1/python-3.6.1-vtgnau5wyennlx7g64w6zc6fk6jnq2ep/lib/python3.6/site-packages/frontmatter/__init__.py", line 155, in dump
    fd.write(content.encode(encoding))
TypeError: string argument expected, got 'bytes'
>>> 
>>> frontmatter.dump(post, bytes_buff)
```

It's because you do [`fd.write(content.encode(encoding))`](https://github.com/eyeseast/python-frontmatter/blob/1b9340744d863658aaa0f765e912c846f909afc4/frontmatter/__init__.py#L155), and `encode` is going to return a `bytes`, and `StringIO` gets mad about that in python3.

I can think of a number of different ways to potentially allow for both, but honestly looking at the rest of your library I think you are _much_ better equipped to decide what the correct action is :wink:

issue_title: GET parameters stripped from URL
issue_body:
In a request like `http+unix://%2Fvar%2Frun%2Fdocker.sock/containers/nginx/logs?timestamp=true`, the GET parameters at the end of the URL are stripped out. This is due to `UnixHTTPConnection.request` only passing through the URL's path to its parent function ([relevant line of code](https://github.com/msabramo/requests-unixsocket/blob/8d692622c9466fcf3d7d4c1c190c7aaf84267c1e/requests_unixsocket/adapters.py#L31)).

The problem is easily fixed by removing the aforementioned line or by removing `UnixHTTPConnection.request` entirely. I'd issue a pull request, but I'm unsure of the motivation behind the current behaviour and want to avoid inadvertently breaking other requests.

issue_title: Hangs on connection with some socket-io clients
issue_body:
I'll reference this bug report for socketIO-client: [Issue 118](https://github.com/invisibleroads/socketIO-client/issues/118).

It appears that if a 'connect' event is captured on the server, and during its execution it emits another event, that some clients will struggle to connect. Tested with both the pythong socket-IO client and a dotnet client.

As 'connect' is a special event, returning False will cause a client to be disconnected. This means that any events that happen in it are added to the packet queue, and only once the event completes does the CONNECT packet get added. These other clients are only expecting the CONNECT and get confused with the event packets coming through.

Removing any emits from my connect handler fixes the python client. And applying a few small patches to handle Content-Type better (depending on b64 flag) fixes the dotnet client as well.

issue_title: pytype for array
issue_body:
Using `pytype` as a type for item of array does not work. 

Example:

    parameters:
        - name: body
          paramType: body
          type: array
          items:
            pytype: MySerializer

issue_title: Provide a way to restore a subset of keys into a running redis server from a RDB File
issue_body:
Very often, due to application bugs, teams lose or corrupt a subset of data within redis. For example, someone may have accidentally deleted some keys or would have run the wrong command. 

There are a few ways to recover data in such cases - a) replaying AOF file after removing the offending command, or b) Restore an older RDB. Replaying the AOF is not always possible, because many cloud providers do not expose the AOF file, and not everyone has AOF enabled. Restoring an older RDB requires developers to spin up a new redis instance and then selectively copy data from the new instance to the existing instance with corrupted data.

This issue proposes another alternative - generating a stream of RESTORE commands. Developers can apply any of the existing filters to select a subset of keys from the RDB file, and then rdbtools will generate appropriate RESTORE commands. We should avoid connecting to redis servers directly. Instead, we should generate a stream of commands, and let the user pipe it to redis server using redis-cli. 

Proposed syntax -
`rdb --c restore /var/redis/6379/dump.rdb`

To restore specific key pattern to a redis server running on localhost
`rdb --c restore --key "users:.*" dump.rdb | redis-cli --pipe`

By default, we will fail if the key already exists. We can pass the `--force` flag to replace existing keys. This will pass the REPLACE flag when invoking the restore command.

issue_title: Inline-serialization
issue_body:
Hi,

I am attempting to serialize an object that has a `backref` to a list (potentially zero, one or many).

```python

class Person(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'person'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(128))
    surname = Column(String(128))


class Score(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'score'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    person_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('person.id'))
    person = relationship('Person', backref='scores')
    value = Column(Integer)
```

This will serialize `Person` like so:

```
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': [{'id': '1', 'person_id': '1', 'value': '1000'},
            {'id': '2', 'person_id': '1', 'value': '2000'}],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

I've tried to find a way to change this serialization to something like this:

```
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': ['1', '2'],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

I don't seem to be able to. It doesn't seem to be possible to change the `typ` of a relationship with the `info` dictionary.

Obviously it is possible to add this:

```
    __colanderalchemy_config__ = {
        'excludes': ['person_id', 'person', 'value']
    }
```

 to reduce the serialization of person to this:

````
{'id': '1',
 'name': u'Jayne',
 'scores': [{'id': '1'}, {'id': '2'}],
 'surname': u'Cobb'}
```

which is a bit better, but not great. However, when I then want to serialize score specifically, it does not include those excluded fields either.

I have create a working example in this gist: https://gist.github.com/dpwrussell/7a72fee067a40f4dca97

Is this impossible?

Cheers

issue_title: BaseObject.__eq__ is incorrect
issue_body:
`BaseObject.__eq__` only compares object ID, not type. So `client.file(id) == client.folder(id)` evaluates to `True`.

It should also return `NotImplemented` if the other object is not a `BaseObject`, but right now it will raise an exception (e.g. #151 ).

This is technically a backwards-incompatible change. We'll include this in the major version bump v2.0.0.

I've started work on this already.

issue_title: Doc: deploying with multiple daphne processes (v1 -> v2)
issue_body:
Hi,

With v1, I could run a single Daphne process and several workers, so that I could bind Daphne directly to a port number.

With v2, the worker and the server share the same Daphne process, so that the number of daphne processes must be increased to reach a similar situation. The Channels doc mentions using a process supervisor to increase the number of processes, but it doesn't state how a port can be shared between the Daphne processes. 

I use Circus (http://circus.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial/step-by-step/) as a process supervisor. Their doc mentions using Circus to create a socket bound to the desired port and to run multiple servers (Chaussette in the case) with the resulting (and common) socket file descriptor. Chaussette's doc explicitly mentions that:

> The particularity of Chaussette is that it can either bind a socket on a port like any other server does or run against already opened sockets.

Is that also true for Daphne? Can Daphne processes share the same socket file descriptor? If yes, could that be mentionned in the doc?

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Daphne health-check
issue_body:
It would be nice for Daphne to support an endpoint that would allow an health check on itself and the channel layer subsystem, with no need for active workers. Maybe send a message on a predefined channel and check if it receives it?

Right now, when we launch the Daphne component, there's no URL that I know of that we can query to check if just the Daphne component is healthy. We need active workers to respond to request, and so it's harder to diagnose a problem.

issue_title: client is logged as "None" when listening to a unix socket
issue_body:
Daphne uses the incoming connection's address and port to determine the `client_addr`, which obviously don't exist when listening to a unix socket. When listening to a unix socket we can assume that there's a reverse proxy in front of Daphne so we can check for "X-Forwarded-For" and "X-Forwarded-Port" headers and log those, if they exist.

Also, I'm not sure it's useful to log client ports. It's almost certainly going to be a high random port in both cases. Looking at other servers for reference, neither apache nor nginx seem to log the client port by deafult.

issue_title: Django >= 1.8 ; Deprecated OptParser usage ArgParse; Warnings on running runserver_plus
issue_body:
Running `runserver_plus` with `Werkzeug` debugger throws the following warning with the latest Django

```
RemovedInDjango110Warning: OptionParser usage for Django management commands is deprecated, use ArgumentParser instead
  RemovedInDjango110Warning)
```

Looks like this is happening because of the usage of `*args` and `**options`,  See the changed in 1.8 which says https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/howto/custom-management-commands/#module-django.core.management
```
Changed in Django 1.8:
Before Django 1.8, management commands were based on the optparse module, and positional 
arguments were passed in *args while optional arguments were passed in **options. Now that 
management commands use argparse for argument parsing, all arguments are passed in **options 
by default, unless you name your positional arguments to args (compatibility mode). You are 
encouraged to exclusively use **options for new commands.
```

issue_title: make query commands work with unwatched roots
issue_body:
This would need to be opt-in I'm guessing.

The motivating factor here is the ability to use Watchman's "query language" without needing to establish temporary watch roots.

Watchman would invisibly take advantage of a watch root if the queried directory is one or a descendant of one. Otherwise, the query will take longer, but I'm okay with that.

The results would include a `watch` property that equals the watch root or null.

issue_title: support watch-del for consolidated roots
issue_body:
When `watch-project` roots are consolidated based on shared `.git` history (is that the only time consolidation occurs?), `watch-del` commands don't work as expected. You're forced to use the common root with `watch-del`, instead of the `watch-project` roots. The problem there is that clients will need to track which `watch-project` roots share a common root, as to avoid sending a premature `watch-del` command.

Watchman could keep a "retain count" for its common roots, allow `watch-del` to always work with `watch-project` roots, and perform the actual `watch-del` job once its retain count hits zero.

issue_title: jQuery migrate 1.2.1 reverts back jQuery Security patches to < 1.9.1
issue_body:
Guys,
For the second time [1] , jQuery Migrate v 1.2.1 reverts back any jQuery to behave like version < 1.9.0
In fact as described on https://code.google.com/p/domxsswiki/wiki/jQuery it's quite known that jQuery was patched to mitigate several XSS potential exposition [2] since version 1.6.1.

At the moment, it seems that jQueryMigrate does not follow the behavios of the different versions of jQuery, and it tries to fix only the jQuery version 1.6.1 pattern /^#.*/.
We would like to have a behavior that is at least expected to the version jQueryMigrate is trying to "migrate"/patch.

Ps. If you ask for a PoC I'll send it privately.

[1] http://blog.mindedsecurity.com/2013/04/jquery-migrate-is-sink-too.html
[2] http://bugs.jquery.com/ticket/9521

Finding content should support sorting.

Version of plone.api: 1.5 (Plone 5.0.6)
